quiz_questions.question_text: Shield volcanoes have gentle slopes because their lava is:

options.text: Extremely high viscosity, which causes it to pile up steeply near the vent
Primarily composed of fragmented volcanic debris and explosive tephra
Rich in dissolved gases that expand rapidly and create lightweight pumice
Low viscosity (fluid), allowing it to flow far before solidifying

quiz_questions.explanation: Shield volcanoes erupt low-viscosity basaltic lava that spreads out in thin, broad layers over long distances before cooling, building wide, gently sloping structures like Mauna Loa in Hawaii.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Hawaiian Islands formed from a:

options.text: Transform boundary
Hot spot
Convergent boundary
Divergent boundary

quiz_questions.explanation: The Hawaiian island chain formed as the Pacific Plate moved over a stationary mantle hot spot, creating a series of volcanic islands with the youngest and most active island (Big Island) currently over the plume.

quiz_questions.question_text: Composite volcanoes (stratovolcanoes) are dangerous because they:

options.text: Never erupt
Only produce slow-moving lava
Can erupt explosively with ash, pyroclastic flows, and lahars
Are the smallest type of volcano

quiz_questions.explanation: Composite volcanoes contain high-viscosity, silica-rich magma that traps dissolved gases, leading to violent explosive eruptions that can produce deadly pyroclastic flows, lahars, and widespread ashfall.

quiz_questions.question_text: Mount St. Helens is an example of a:

options.text: Composite volcano (stratovolcano)
Cinder cone
Shield volcano
Hot spot volcano

quiz_questions.explanation: Mount St. Helens is a composite volcano (stratovolcano) in the Cascade Range, known for its catastrophic 1980 explosive eruption that blew away the mountain's north face.

quiz_questions.question_text: High-viscosity magma tends to produce _____ eruptions.

options.text: Underwater only
Gentle, effusive
No eruptions at all
Explosive

quiz_questions.explanation: High-viscosity magma traps dissolved gases because the thick magma prevents bubbles from escaping, and when pressure is released, the trapped gas expands violently, causing explosive eruptions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Volcanic eruptions can affect global climate by:

options.text: Absorbing incoming solar radiation in the lower atmosphere and converting it to long-wave infrared heat energy
Opening deep rifts in the ocean floor that allow cold abyssal water to circulate and cool surface temperatures
Releasing heavy volcanic particles that settle in the troposphere and produce no lasting effect on global climate
Releasing sulfur dioxide and ash into the stratosphere, reflecting sunlight and cooling temperatures

quiz_questions.explanation: Volcanic sulfur dioxide injected into the stratosphere forms sulfate aerosol particles that reflect incoming sunlight back into space, causing temporary global cooling that can last one to two years.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cinder cone volcanoes are typically:

options.text: Completely flat
Small, steep-sided, and built from ejected lava fragments
The largest type of volcano
Found only underwater

quiz_questions.explanation: Cinder cone volcanoes are the smallest and simplest type, built from fragments of lava (cinder and ash) ejected from a single vent that pile up into a steep, cone-shaped hill.

quiz_questions.question_text: Yellowstone is an example of a _____ hot spot.

options.text: Man-made
Continental
Oceanic
Extinct

quiz_questions.explanation: Yellowstone is a continental hot spot where a mantle plume beneath the North American continental crust produces geysers, hot springs, and a supervolcano caldera with potential for massive eruptions.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo cooled global temperatures by about:

options.text: 5 degrees Celsius permanently
0.5 degrees Celsius for 1 to 2 years
0 degrees Celsius (no effect)
10 degrees Celsius for a decade

quiz_questions.explanation: Mount Pinatubo's eruption injected approximately 20 million tons of sulfur dioxide into the stratosphere, creating a global aerosol layer that cooled average temperatures by about 0.5 degrees Celsius for one to two years.

quiz_questions.question_text: Volcanic soil is often very fertile because:

options.text: It is extremely old and well-developed
Volcanic ash weathers into mineral-rich soil with nutrients like potassium and phosphorus
It contains large amounts of water
It is always located in tropical regions

quiz_questions.explanation: Volcanic ash is rich in minerals and weathers rapidly into nutrient-dense soil containing potassium, phosphorus, calcium, and other elements essential for plant growth, explaining why volcanic regions often have productive agriculture.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is viscosity in the context of volcanic eruptions?

options.text: The internal temperature of molten rock measured in degrees, which determines whether an eruption will be explosive or effusive in character
The visible color spectrum of erupted lava on the surface, ranging from dark basaltic black to light rhyolitic gray depending on mineral content
The total volume of dissolved gas trapped within magma before eruption, which controls the force and height of the resulting volcanic ash column
A measure of a fluid's resistance to flow; high-viscosity magma is thick and flows slowly, while low-viscosity magma is thin and flows easily

quiz_questions.explanation: Viscosity measures a fluid's resistance to flow; high-viscosity magma (thick, like honey) traps gas and erupts explosively, while low-viscosity magma (thin, like water) allows gas to escape and flows gently.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a hot spot?

options.text: A fixed area of intense volcanic activity caused by a mantle plume rising from deep within Earth, independent of plate boundaries
A region along a convergent plate boundary where two tectonic plates collide and generate volcanic eruptions through crustal compression forces
An area of exceptionally high surface air temperature in arid desert regions caused by intense solar radiation and very low cloud cover
A persistent warm ocean current that flows along continental coastlines and transfers tropical heat energy toward polar regions over time

quiz_questions.explanation: A hot spot is a volcanic region fed by a mantle plume, a column of abnormally hot rock rising from deep in the mantle, that remains relatively stationary while tectonic plates move over it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a pyroclastic flow?

options.text: A fast-moving avalanche of hot gas, ash, and rock fragments that can exceed 100 km/h and 700 degrees Celsius
A slow-moving stream of lava
A flow of water mixed with volcanic mud
A gentle emission of steam from a volcanic vent

quiz_questions.explanation: A pyroclastic flow is one of the most lethal volcanic hazards, consisting of a fast-moving current of superheated gas and volcanic debris that can travel at over 100 km/h with temperatures exceeding 700 degrees Celsius.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a lahar?

options.text: A destructive mudflow composed of volcanic debris and water that flows rapidly down volcanic slopes
A type of dense volcanic rock formed when lava cools rapidly under high-pressure conditions deep underground
A seismic event triggered by magma movement beneath a volcano that causes the surrounding ground to shake
A cloud of toxic volcanic gases including sulfur dioxide and carbon dioxide released from an active vent

quiz_questions.explanation: A lahar is a volcanic mudflow or debris flow made of a slurry of volcanic material and water that can travel rapidly down valleys, burying communities with a concrete-like mixture of ash, rock, and water.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'magma chamber' mean?

options.text: A large underground pool of molten rock beneath a volcano that feeds eruptions when pressure builds sufficiently
A hollow cavity deep underground that is filled with pressurized volcanic gases which may vent through fumaroles at the surface
The large bowl-shaped depression at the summit of a volcano formed by the collapse of rock after previous major eruptions
A specially equipped research facility where volcanologists monitor seismic activity and carefully analyze volcanic rock samples

quiz_questions.explanation: A magma chamber is a large reservoir of molten rock beneath a volcano, typically located several kilometers below the surface, that stores magma until enough pressure builds to force it upward in an eruption.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a caldera?

options.text: A broad, slow-moving type of lava flow that spreads across wide areas of flat terrain before cooling into a thin layer of basalt
A volcanic island that forms gradually as eruptions from an oceanic hot spot build layers of basaltic lava above the sea surface
A small secondary volcanic vent on the flanks of a larger volcano that intermittently releases steam, gases, and minor lava flows
A large, basin-shaped volcanic depression formed when a volcano collapses into its emptied magma chamber after a massive eruption

quiz_questions.explanation: A caldera is a large depression formed when the roof of a magma chamber collapses inward after a large volume of magma has been evacuated during an eruption, such as the Yellowstone Caldera.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'mantle plume' mean?

options.text: A hollow underground tunnel formed by flowing lava beneath the surface of a shield volcano that channels molten rock to distant vents
A deep fracture in the ocean floor along a divergent plate boundary where tectonic plates are slowly spreading apart from one another
A column of abnormally hot rock rising from deep within Earth's mantle that can create volcanic hot spots at the surface
A towering column of volcanic ash and fine particles ejected high into the atmosphere during an explosive stratovolcano eruption event

quiz_questions.explanation: A mantle plume is a column of exceptionally hot rock rising from the deep mantle, possibly from near the core-mantle boundary, that creates localized volcanic activity at the surface independent of plate boundaries.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'effusive eruption' mean?

options.text: A relatively gentle eruption where low-viscosity lava flows steadily from a vent rather than exploding
A violently explosive eruption with ash clouds
An eruption that occurs underwater only
A volcanic eruption that produces only gas

quiz_questions.explanation: An effusive eruption is characterized by the relatively gentle outpouring of low-viscosity lava that flows freely from a vent, typical of shield volcanoes like those in Hawaii.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'geothermal energy' mean?

options.text: Kinetic energy released during earthquakes as seismic waves travel through Earth's crust and cause widespread ground shaking
Chemical potential energy stored within volcanic gases such as sulfur dioxide and hydrogen sulfide at active volcanic vents
Heat energy derived from Earth's interior that can be harnessed for electricity generation and direct heating
Solar radiation absorbed and stored in exposed surface rocks, then gradually released as radiant heat during the nighttime hours

quiz_questions.explanation: Geothermal energy is heat from Earth's interior, particularly abundant near volcanic areas and hot spots, that can be captured through wells and used to generate electricity or directly heat buildings.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'volcanic explosivity index (VEI)' mean?

options.text: A scale from 0 to 8 that measures the relative explosiveness of volcanic eruptions based on volume of material ejected and eruption column height
A measurement of the velocity at which lava flows downslope from a volcanic vent, calculated using the lava's viscosity and terrain steepness
A quantitative measure of erupted lava temperature in degrees Celsius, used to classify volcanic eruptions by their total thermal output
A regional count of all currently active volcanic vents within a defined geographic area, updated annually by geological survey agencies

quiz_questions.explanation: The VEI is a scale from 0 (gentle effusive) to 8 (mega-colossal) that classifies eruptions based on the volume of erupted material, eruption cloud height, and duration, similar to the Richter scale for earthquakes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A volcanologist monitoring a volcano in Indonesia notices that the magma has high silica content. She immediately raises the alert level. A colleague from Hawaii, where volcanoes typically have low-silica magma, asks why high silica is so dangerous. What is the correct explanation?

options.text: High silica content increases magma viscosity, trapping gases that build pressure and lead to violent explosive eruptions
High silica only affects the color of the lava, not its behavior
High silica makes the lava flow faster
High silica makes the volcano taller

quiz_questions.explanation: High silica content makes magma very viscous (thick), preventing dissolved gases from escaping; as the magma rises and pressure decreases, the trapped gas expands violently, causing explosive eruptions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A geologist studying the Hawaiian island chain notices that the islands get progressively older from southeast to northwest. The Big Island (youngest) is currently volcanically active, while the northwestern islands are extinct and eroded. What mechanism explains this age progression?

options.text: Each island was formed by a different volcanic eruption at the same time
The hot spot moves southeast, creating new islands in front of it
Ocean currents erode the western islands faster
The Pacific Plate has been moving northwest over a stationary hot spot, so older islands have been carried away from the magma source

quiz_questions.explanation: The Pacific Plate moves northwest over the stationary Hawaiian hot spot, so each island was built while over the plume and then carried away, with the youngest island currently above the active magma source.

quiz_questions.question_text: A city emergency manager near Mount Rainier in Washington state is most concerned about lahars, not lava flows. She explains to the city council that lahars pose a greater threat to surrounding communities. Why are lahars especially dangerous for cities near snow-capped stratovolcanoes?

options.text: Lahars create poisonous gas that spreads over wide areas
Lahars are hotter than lava and burn everything in their path
Lahars only form in tropical volcanic regions
Volcanic heat melts glaciers and snow, mixing with volcanic debris to create fast-moving mudflows that can travel far down river valleys to populated areas

quiz_questions.explanation: Mount Rainier's massive glaciers can melt during volcanic activity, mixing with ash and rock to form lahars that can travel down river valleys at high speed for tens of kilometers, reaching populated lowland areas.

quiz_questions.question_text: A historian studying the eruption of Mount Tambora in 1815 explains that the following year (1816) was called 'The Year Without a Summer' because global temperatures dropped and crops failed across Europe and North America. What volcanic process caused this worldwide cooling?

options.text: The eruption caused an ice age by altering ocean currents
Volcanic ash fell directly on crops worldwide
The eruption blocked river water from reaching farmland
Massive amounts of sulfur dioxide and ash injected into the stratosphere formed an aerosol layer that reflected sunlight and reduced global temperatures

quiz_questions.explanation: Tambora's massive eruption (VEI 7) injected enormous quantities of sulfur dioxide into the stratosphere, forming sulfate aerosols that reflected sunlight globally, reducing temperatures enough to cause crop failures.

quiz_questions.question_text: A renewable energy company is evaluating Iceland as a location for a geothermal power plant. They note that Iceland already generates about 25% of its electricity from geothermal sources. Why is Iceland so well-suited for geothermal energy production?

options.text: Iceland's cold climate concentrates geothermal energy
Iceland sits on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge where hot mantle material rises close to the surface, providing abundant accessible underground heat
Iceland is located near the equator where it is warmest
Iceland has more underground coal deposits than other countries

quiz_questions.explanation: Iceland straddles the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, a divergent boundary where hot mantle material rises close to the surface, making geothermal reservoirs shallow and easily accessible for power generation and heating.

quiz_questions.question_text: A tourist visiting Hawaii watches a slow lava flow from Kilauea enter the ocean, creating new land. She compares this to news footage of Mount Pinatubo's explosive eruption. A park ranger explains why the two volcanoes behave so differently. What is the key factor?

options.text: Kilauea has a much smaller magma chamber than Pinatubo, so it cannot build enough gas pressure for the violent explosive eruptions typical of Philippine volcanoes
Kilauea formed more recently than Pinatubo and has not yet developed the deep magma reservoir needed to produce the violent explosive eruptions of older volcanoes
Kilauea erupts low-viscosity basaltic lava that flows easily, while Pinatubo erupts high-viscosity, silica-rich magma that traps gas and explodes violently
Pacific Ocean water surrounding Hawaii constantly cools Kilauea's erupting lava and absorbs the thermal energy that would otherwise fuel explosive volcanic activity

quiz_questions.explanation: Magma composition determines eruption style: Kilauea's basaltic magma is low in silica and viscosity, allowing gas to escape easily for gentle eruptions, while Pinatubo's silica-rich magma traps gas for explosive eruptions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A farmer living near an active volcano in Java, Indonesia, refuses to relocate despite eruption risks. She explains that her volcanic soil produces exceptional rice crops. A geologist confirms her reasoning. Why is soil near active volcanoes often among the most productive agricultural land?

options.text: Fresh volcanic ash weathers rapidly into mineral-rich soil packed with nutrients like potassium, phosphorus, and calcium that promote plant growth
Volcanic regions receive more rainfall than other areas
Volcanic heat warms the soil and helps plants grow faster
Volcanic gases fertilize the soil directly

quiz_questions.explanation: Volcanic ash is rich in essential plant nutrients and weathers much faster than most rocks, creating fertile soils in decades rather than centuries, which is why some of the world's most productive farmland surrounds active volcanoes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A geology professor asks students to explain why Yellowstone's hot spot has created a chain of progressively older volcanic features stretching across Idaho to the northeast. A student suggests the hot spot has been moving southwest. The professor corrects her. What is actually happening?

options.text: Multiple separate hot spots have formed in a line
The North American Plate is moving southwest over the stationary hot spot, so older volcanic features are found to the northeast where the plate used to be
Earthquakes have shifted the volcanic features to the northeast
The hot spot moves southwest while the plate stays still

quiz_questions.explanation: The mantle plume beneath Yellowstone is stationary, but the North American Plate moves southwest over it, so older volcanic calderas and features are found to the northeast along the Snake River Plain in Idaho.

quiz_questions.question_text: A disaster planner is creating an eruption response plan for a community near a composite volcano. She reviews the most common volcanic hazards: lava flows, pyroclastic flows, lahars, ashfall, and volcanic gases. Which hazard can travel the farthest from the volcano and affect the largest area?

options.text: Lava flows, because their extreme temperatures melt and consume everything in their path for hundreds of kilometers before the molten rock cools enough to solidify
Ashfall, because fine volcanic ash can be carried hundreds to thousands of kilometers by wind, affecting air quality, agriculture, and transportation over vast areas
Volcanic gases, because their lightweight molecular composition enables them to spread in all directions from the vent and travel great distances across entire continents
Pyroclastic flows, because their extreme speed of over 700 km/h allows them to travel enormous distances from the volcano, devastating everything along their path

quiz_questions.explanation: While pyroclastic flows and lahars are deadlier locally, volcanic ashfall can be carried by prevailing winds for hundreds to thousands of kilometers, disrupting aviation, damaging crops, and causing respiratory problems over enormous areas.

quiz_questions.question_text: A volcanologist monitoring seismic activity beneath a dormant volcano detects increasing numbers of small earthquakes and ground uplift measured by GPS sensors. She warns that an eruption may be approaching. A local official asks how earthquakes beneath a volcano can predict an eruption. What is the scientific basis for this warning?

options.text: Earthquakes always cause volcanic eruptions
Rising magma fractures surrounding rock as it pushes toward the surface, causing increasing numbers of small earthquakes and measurable ground deformation
Seismic activity has no relationship to volcanic eruptions
The earthquakes are caused by tourists walking near the volcano

quiz_questions.explanation: As magma rises through the crust, it forces rock apart and fractures surrounding material, generating swarms of small earthquakes and causing the ground surface to inflate, both of which are reliable precursors to volcanic eruptions.
